skill.name: metasploit-framework
skill.description: Guides users through Metasploit Framework workflows, including exploit selection, payload generation, post-exploitation, and auxiliary scanning.
skill.summary: Provides hands-on Metasploit Framework guidance for offensive security workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings are confirmed as high-risk offensive security instructions, not only benign documentation. Although the skill mentions authorization, it provides operational guidance for exploitation, reverse payload generation, credential harvesting, keylogging, screenshots, persistence, and AV evasion. The markdown backtick detections are false positives as code execution in the skill file, but the commands inside those blocks are dangerous user-facing instructions.

critical_findings.title: End-to-End Exploitation Workflow
critical_findings.description: The skill instructs users to select Metasploit exploit modules, configure targets and payloads, verify vulnerability, and run exploitation. This enables unauthorized system compromise if used outside an approved lab or engagement.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections explicitly name exploit modules, payloads, and commands to execute exploitation. The surrounding purpose text confirms the content is intended for active offensive use.

critical_findings.title: Post-Exploitation Credential Theft and Surveillance
critical_findings.description: The skill provides Meterpreter and post module commands for file transfer, privilege escalation, hash dumping, credential collection, screenshots, and keylogging. These are direct compromise and data theft capabilities.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text directly includes credential harvesting, hash dumping, screenshots, and keyscan commands. These are unambiguous post-compromise abuse primitives.

critical_findings.title: Malware Payload Generation and Evasion Guidance
critical_findings.description: The skill shows msfvenom commands to create reverse Meterpreter payloads for Windows, Linux, PHP, Python, PowerShell, ASP, WAR, Android, and encoded executables. It also advises evasion and AV detection bypass.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands generate standalone reverse payloads and encoded executables. The troubleshooting guidance explicitly recommends evasion modules and encoding when payloads are detected.

high_findings.title: Network Scanning and Brute Force Instructions
high_findings.description: The skill includes auxiliary module workflows for SMB, SSH, FTP, HTTP directory scanning, port scanning, and SSH password brute forcing. This can facilitate reconnaissance and credential attacks against live networks.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section directly configures scanners and an SSH login module with user and password files. The hardcoded IPs are private placeholders, but the workflow is operational.

high_findings.title: Persistence and Pivoting Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill lists post-exploitation modules for executable persistence, SSH key persistence, autoroute pivoting, and port forwarding. These behaviors can extend unauthorized access and movement through a network.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited commands and modules explicitly support persistence and pivoting after compromise. These features have legitimate lab uses but are high-risk in marketplace distribution.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Installation and Service Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to download an installer, mark it executable, run it, start PostgreSQL, and initialize the Metasploit database with sudo. These commands are expected for Metasploit setup but increase local system risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The setup commands are legitimate Metasploit installation steps, but they download code and use sudo. This is risky but secondary to the confirmed offensive workflows.

medium_findings.title: File Transfer Instructions on Compromised Hosts
medium_findings.description: The Meterpreter workflow includes downloading from a target and uploading tools to a target. This can support data exfiltration or tool staging during unauthorized access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file transfer commands are explicitly post-exploitation operations. They are dangerous when combined with the exploit and session workflows elsewhere in the skill.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Backticks Are Not Executed
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many markdown backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are false positives for direct code execution because SKILL.md is documentation, not executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backticks are markdown formatting around examples and command names. The safety issue comes from the instructed commands, not from automatic execution by the skill file.

low_findings.title: Private IP Addresses Are Example Targets
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP address detections use private RFC1918 ranges as examples. They are not evidence of exfiltration endpoints, but they appear in exploit, scanner, handler, and payload instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses are private sample values, so the hardcoded-IP pattern itself is mostly a false positive. The surrounding commands remain dangerous because they operationalize attacks.

dangerous_patterns.title: Exploit Execution Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples direct users to configure Metasploit modules and execute run or exploit against a selected target.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit exploit execution steps. There is no ambiguity that the workflow launches modules against targets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Meterpreter Keylogging and Screenshot Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands for taking screenshots and starting, dumping, and stopping keylogging from a Meterpreter session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact commands are shown in both workflow and quick reference sections. Screen capture and keylogging are clear surveillance capabilities.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Harvesting Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes hashdump and credential collection modules for extracting credentials from compromised systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The named commands and modules collect password hashes and credentials. This is a direct data theft pattern when misused.

dangerous_patterns.title: Reverse Payload Generation
dangerous_patterns.description: The msfvenom examples generate reverse Meterpreter payloads across several platforms and file formats.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands create executable or script payload artifacts that call back to an operator listener. This is a classic malware delivery pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Detection Evasion Advice
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill discusses encoders, evasion modules, and avoiding detection by monitoring systems.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple sections reference evasion, encoding, and avoiding detection. This materially increases abuse potential beyond ordinary vulnerability validation.

content.value_statement: Security teams need structured reviews of Metasploit workflows before using them in controlled assessments. This skill explains Metasploit console usage, payload choices, auxiliary modules, and post-exploitation steps.
actual_capabilities: Explains Metasploit module categories and common console commands.
Shows how to search for modules by name, CVE, platform, type, and rank.
Describes exploit configuration fields such as RHOSTS, RPORT, payload, LHOST, and LPORT.
Documents auxiliary scanners for SMB, SSH, FTP, HTTP directories, and port scanning.
Lists msfvenom payload formats and handler setup steps.
Summarizes Meterpreter session, post-exploitation, and troubleshooting commands.
limitations: The skill is high risk because it includes exploitation and post-exploitation instructions.
It does not verify user authorization or enforce scope boundaries.
It does not provide defensive guardrails beyond brief legal reminders.
It should not be published for general marketplace use without major safety redesign.

use_cases.title: Review lab-only training material
use_cases.description: A security instructor can evaluate whether Metasploit examples are limited to an isolated lab.

use_cases.title: Audit offensive workflow content
use_cases.description: A marketplace reviewer can identify exploit, payload, credential, persistence, and evasion content.

use_cases.title: Map assessment procedures
use_cases.description: A red team lead can compare documented Metasploit steps against an approved rules of engagement document.

prompt_templates.title: Identify covered topics
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of the skill scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize the Metasploit topics covered by this skill. Separate setup, module selection, payload generation, scanning, and post-exploitation.

prompt_templates.title: Check authorization language
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this skill for authorization and scope safeguards. Identify missing controls that would reduce misuse risk.

prompt_templates.title: Classify risky capabilities
prompt_templates.prompt: Classify each capability in this skill by risk: scanning, exploitation, payload generation, credential access, persistence, evasion, and data access.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a safer rewrite plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a remediation plan that converts this Metasploit skill into defensive, lab-only training content without operational abuse instructions.

output_examples.output: The review identifies critical risk because the skill includes exploit execution, payload generation, keylogging, credential collection, persistence, and evasion guidance.

output_examples.input: Summarize what the skill can do.
output: It explains Metasploit console navigation and module categories.
It documents exploit configuration, auxiliary scanning, payload generation, handlers, and Meterpreter sessions.
It includes post-exploitation operations that require strict authorization.

output_examples.output: Position the content as defensive review guidance only. Remove executable attack steps, payload generation, credential collection, keylogging, persistence, and evasion instructions.

best_practices: Use only in isolated labs or engagements with written authorization.
Replace operational exploit steps with defensive explanations and review checklists.
Document scope, target ownership, logging, cleanup, and approval before any assessment.
anti_patterns: Do not publish unrestricted guidance for payload generation, keylogging, persistence, or evasion.
Do not use example targets, listeners, or credentials against systems outside approved scope.
Do not treat authorization disclaimers as sufficient safety controls.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: No. The audit marks it critical because it includes actionable exploitation and post-exploitation guidance.

faq.question: Why is Metasploit content high risk?
faq.answer: Metasploit can execute exploits, generate payloads, open sessions, collect credentials, and support persistence.

faq.question: Does the skill include legal warnings?
faq.answer: Yes, but warnings do not remove the risk from operational attack instructions.

faq.question: Can it be used for legitimate security work?
faq.answer: Yes, but only under written authorization and strict scope controls.

faq.question: What should be removed for marketplace approval?
faq.answer: Remove payload generation, exploit execution, credential collection, keylogging, persistence, evasion, and brute force instructions.

faq.question: Does the audit find prompt injection?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the reviewed SKILL.md sections.
